coredata.dc:title: Lignocellulosic biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs): A novel approach utilizing organosolv lignin from Moso bamboo waste

coredata.dc:description: Converting lignocellulosic materials to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has garnered significant interest to transform biomass wastes into highly valuable products. We chose to use lignin as our feedstock because high-value products more readily utilize cellulose and hemicellulose. Lignin, arguably the most underutilized and complex biopolymer within any biomass, presents an appealing yet overlooked opportunity. While some may argue that lignin-based CQDs are no longer groundbreaking, this is because existing studies typically rely on commercially available lignin, ignoring biomass-derived sources. Therefore, in our pioneering research, we utilized organosolv lignin, prized for its structural purity and extracted from Moso bamboo waste, as the carbon source in a facile two-step technique to synthesize luminous CQDs. The process involved the addition of 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, a mild-organic acid that acted as a source for both nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S). A 12-h hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C with 0.1 M of acid addition attained the best result. The resultant CQDs display strong green fluorescence, with the highest quantum yield of 17.7% seen in N and S co-doped CQDs. With their obvious and specific fluorescence quenching impact on Fe3+, CQDs demonstrate great potential as a fluorescent nanoprobe for metal ion detection. This CQD nanoprobe responds very sensitively to Fe3+ between 0 and 500 μM (R2 = 0.9803), with a detection limit of 0.15 μM. This research not only presents a unique alternative nanoprobe for the sensing field using renewable biomass lignin, but it also paves the way for the long-term, cost-effective, and scalable manufacturing of CQDs.

head.ce:title: Lignocellulosic biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs): A novel approach utilizing organosolv lignin from Moso bamboo waste

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Converting lignocellulosic materials to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has garnered significant interest to transform biomass wastes into highly valuable products. We chose to use lignin as our feedstock because high-value products more readily utilize cellulose and hemicellulose. Lignin, arguably the most underutilized and complex biopolymer within any biomass, presents an appealing yet overlooked opportunity. While some may argue that lignin-based CQDs are no longer groundbreaking, this is because existing studies typically rely on commercially available lignin, ignoring biomass-derived sources. Therefore, in our pioneering research, we utilized organosolv lignin, prized for its structural purity and extracted from Moso bamboo waste, as the carbon source in a facile two-step technique to synthesize luminous CQDs. The process involved the addition of 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, a mild-organic acid that acted as a source for both nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S). A 12-h hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C with 0.1 M of acid addition attained the best result. The resultant CQDs display strong green fluorescence, with the highest quantum yield of 17.7% seen in N and S co-doped CQDs. With their obvious and specific fluorescence quenching impact on Fe3+, CQDs demonstrate great potential as a fluorescent nanoprobe for metal ion detection. This CQD nanoprobe responds very sensitively to Fe3+ between 0 and 500 μM (R2 = 0.9803), with a detection limit of 0.15 μM. This research not only presents a unique alternative nanoprobe for the sensing field using renewable biomass lignin, but it also paves the way for the long-term, cost-effective, and scalable manufacturing of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The production of energy and materials from lignocellulosic biomass (LCB), a renewable and carbon-neutral resource in nature, has been at the forefront of recent efforts to phase out the use of fossil-based resources (Ahmad Farid et al., 2019; Ibrahim et al., 2021). LCB consists of polymers of carbohydrates: cellulose (40–43%), hemicelluloses (28–35%), and lignin (22–29%) (Sharma et al., 2021). The highly valued utilization of cellulose and hemicellulose in the production of ethanol and chemicals has achieved major advances, but the successful conversion of lignin remains a major challenge due to its complexity and recalcitrance (Nagarajan et al., 2017). A staggering 98% of lignin is either disposed of in streams as black liquor or incinerated as a heat source (Mäki et al., 2021). C–C and C–O bonds link the p-hydroxyphenol (H), guaiacyl (G), and syringyl (S) phenylpropane units that make up the three-dimensional (3D) structure of amorphous lignin (Constant et al., 2016). One of lignin's cutting-edge applications is in the eco-friendly production of aromatic carbon nanomaterials, which benefit from the natural structure's characteristic aromatic rings (Ahmad Farid et al., 2023b, 2023d).

The prevailing view on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) architecture is that it features aromatic polymerization of quasi-spherical graphene nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm and an abundant oxygen moiety at the edges (Zhao et al., 2020). Recent initiatives have attempted to produce advanced carbon nanomaterials by utilizing biopolymers available in lignocellulosic biomass as precursors (Ahmad Farid and Andou, 2022; Zakaria et al., 2023). Intriguingly, lignin's structure is comparable to that of CQDs since both include several aromatic rings and oxygen-containing functional groups, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, methoxy, and others. Lignin unquestionably emerges as an ideal raw material for CQDs, providing an alternative avenue for harnessing abundant biomass resources.

Hydrothermal carbonization has been the preferred method for producing CQDs due to its pragmatic, inexpensive, and ecologically benign nature. Nevertheless, this simple one-pot procedure often results in poor brightness and low quantum yield, limiting their applicability as fluorescent materials (Zhao et al., 2021). Doping native CQDs' characteristics has been proven to improve their photoluminescence (PL) performance, particularly with the addition of acid additives. These additives serve both as hydrotropes and dopants (Wang et al., 2019). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) has been the acid of interest in one-pot hydrothermal processes, showing exceptional selectivity and sensitivity in CQDs for the detection of Sudan I, with a quantum yield (QY) of 13.5% (Yang et al., 2020). In an alternative approach, nitric acid (HNO3) was utilized through a two-step procedure involving sonication and hydrothermal treatment, where the resulting CQDs exhibited a notable QY of 21%, making them favourable for bioimaging applications (Ding et al., 2018). However, as suggested by Zhu et al. (2021a), consider opting for less corrosive and environmentally friendly chemicals, such as organic acids, as a safer and more benign option. Furthermore, incorporating dual dopants, such as 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, acting as a source for both nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S), can markedly enhance the PL performance of the CQDs with just a single addition of organic acid. Empirical evidence has demonstrated that the inclusion of 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid shows the most optimal fluorescence QY compared to other organic acids, such as 4-aminobenzoic acid, benzenesulfonic acid, and 4-aminobenzene sulfonic acid (Zhu et al., 2021b), thereby highlighting its practical relevance for our research objectives.

We differentiate our work by extracting organosolv lignin from Moso bamboo, a methodology we detailed in our prior publication (Ahmad Farid et al., 2023a). Compared to traditional methods such as kraft pulping, the organosolv approach produces lignin with higher purity and a lower molecular weight, enhancing its suitability for downstream processing and value-added applications. Moreover, the process is environmentally friendlier, employing diluted organic solvents such as ethanol or methanol along with mild acid catalysts, resulting in reduced emissions of hazardous chemicals and lower energy consumption. Deriving CQDs from organosolv lignin exemplifies sustainable practices by tapping into a renewable and abundant biomass resource, a novel approach not previously reported, contributing to the reduction of waste and reliance on fossil-based materials. Additionally, our research introduces a novel two-step technique for synthesizing N and S co-doped CQDs employing 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as a mild organic acid additive. Mild organic acids, because they are non-toxic and cost-effective, reduce hazardous waste generation, making them appealing for industrial-scale CQD production.

This research not only demonstrates the practical relevance of utilizing this additive to enhance fluorescence quantum yield, but also explores its potential application as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of ferric ions (Fe3+), a trivalent water contaminant. This is due to the superior detection capabilities of CQD-based fluorescent nanoprobes, which offer heightened sensitivity, improved accuracy, and faster reaction times compared to traditional methods such as UV spectroscopy, complexometric titration, and atomic absorption spectroscopy (Asadi et al., 2021). Despite the existence of CQD-based nanoprobes for metal ion detection in previous studies (F. Yang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2016; Q. Yang et al., 2018), there remains a demand for the development of more sustainable and cost-effective alternatives without compromising detection performance. Therefore, we expect the objectives of this study to align with the need for high-performing, low-cost CQDs generated from readily available lignocellulosic biomass with minimal modification.

Ethanol (C2H5OH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (C6H8N2O3S), iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl₂·4H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), copper (II) sulphate pentahydrate (CuSO4⋅5H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), lead (II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), and Spectrapore 7 dialysis membrane (3500 da) were purchased from Fuji Film Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Ltd., Japan. Deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (99.9%, DMSO‑d 6) and sodium chloride (80%, NaClO2) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Japan. Quinine sulphate dihydrate (C40H54N4O10S) was purchased from Nacalai Tesque, Japan. Bamboo biomass (ground Moso bamboo) was supplied by Sanwa Co. Ltd., based in Kitakyushu, Japan.

The ground Moso bamboo was hydrolyzed by mixing with 1 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) and 70% (v/v) ethanol in a pressure-proof container at a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1:20. The container was covered firmly to minimize moisture loss and ethanol evaporation throughout the 1-h hydrothermal treatment in an autoclave (TOMY SX-500, produced by Tomy Digital Biology Co., Ltd. in Japan). After bringing the resultant mixture to room temperature, it was vacuum-filtered (Buchner, UK) to remove the solid residue, and then the liquid fraction's alcohol was recovered using evaporation (EXSTAR6000, Hitachi, Japan). To extract lignin, the first black liquor was diluted to a concentration of 1:10 with distilled water. Next, the mixture was vacuum-filtered and dried at 65 °C for the whole night. To circumvent redundancy, the extensive elucidation of the physicochemical characteristics of the fractionated lignin has been previously addressed in our research (Ahmad Farid et al., 2023a).

The two-step process adopted to synthesize CQDs from organosolv lignin was outlined by Zhu et al. (2021a), with some adjustments made to the parameters under which the reaction took place. Carefully adding the acid (0.0125–0.1 M) to 30 mL of distilled water (DI) in the organosolv lignin (0.3 g) was the first step. The mixture was homogenized through ultrasonication for 20 min before being transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor. The hydrothermal reaction proceeded at temperatures ranging from 110 to 200 °C for durations spanning 1.5–24 h. At room temperature, the resulting CQDs were recovered from centrifugation (9500 rpm, 20 min) and subsequently filtered using a PVDF filter membrane (0.45 μm) to remove any coarser particles. Three days were spent dialyzing the leftover filtrate against DI water in a dialysis bag to remove residual acids and/or other contaminants. After 12 h of freeze-drying at −60 °C, the aqueous solution of as-dialyzed CQDs was dehydrated to powder, which was then collected for characterization and performance evaluations. The CQDs were developed without and with an acid additive consisting of 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid; these variants were designated as B-CQDs and M-CQDs, respectively.

Morphology was determined using a transmission electron microscope at 200 kV (JEOL, Peabody, MA, USA). All samples were ultrasonically treated before being mounted on copper grids for examination. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. The XRD pattern was analysed using a 40 kV, 44 mA Rigaku Ultima III instrument, scanning at 10° min−1 between 20 and 80° with Cu–K radiation at 1.541 Å.

A JASCO NRS-5100 Laser Raman Spectrometer was used to take the Raman spectroscopy measurements (JASCO International Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). A 532 nm excitation wavelength and a 2.5 mW power beam were used. The relative intensity ratio between the D band (about 1350 cm−1) and the G band (around 1580 cm−1) was examined to learn more about the development of graphitic domains.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed using an Axis Nova surface analyser and an Al Kα X-ray source in this research (Kratos Analytical Ltd., Manchester, UK). The Gaussian-Lorentzian curve fitting against the Shirley background was used to determine the relative abundance of each element type (Version 2.3.22rev1.0I, Casa Software Ltd., Teignmouth, UK). In order to evaluate catalyst surface functionalities, we employed potassium bromide (KBr) in FTIR spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer, model 100 series). The spectra were collected at room temperature in the range of 4000-650 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield (QY) and band gap calculations
ce:section.ce:para:
This UV–vis spectrum was gathered from 200 to 700 nm using a Yuanxi UV-5200 spectrophotometer. The spectra of PL's excitation and emission were acquired using a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. With quinine sulphate (in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4; φ = 0.54) as a standard, the quantum yield (φ) of CQDs was calculated. The QY of each sample's optimum excitation wavelength was determined using Eq. (1).

The direct band gap energy (Eg) of CQDs can be determined through Tauc plots of (αℎν)1/r versus (ℎν) derived from Eq. (2) using UV–Vis spectra. The linear segments of the curves are extrapolated to the energy axis at (αℎν)1/r = 0. The absorption coefficient (α) is obtained from the relationship I/I0 e−αd (Beer–Lambert law), α = 2.302 A/d, where d is the path length of the quartz cuvette and A is the absorbance determined from the UV–visible spectrum.

Solutions ranging in pH from 2.0 to 12.0 were made using 50 mM HCl and NaOH. Using a pH-adjusted solution, 2 ml of 0.1 mg mL−1 M-CQD solution was made and evenly mixed at room temperature for 15 min. The spectra of fluorescence emission from these solutions were then determined (Liu et al., 2021).

Pioneering in using biomass-sourced lignin as the carbonaceous precursor for CQDs, our study focused primarily on evaluating CQDs in a pure water sample to establish a baseline. Evaluation of CQDs in pure water enables the isolation of specific factors, such as pH or the presence of extraneous substances, thereby minimizing experimental variability in their PL characteristics. This controlled environment eliminates the confounding effects of complex matrices, as often found in wastewater, such as sewage and industrial effluent, with varying solution characteristics, thereby buttressing their reproducibility and helping to elucidate the underlying mechanisms governing their behaviour.

For the detection specificity test, 20 μL of metal ion solution were added to 2 ml of CQDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1). The fluorescence emissions were taken after the mixture rested for 15 min at room temperature and was well stirred, ensuring consistent environmental conditions and homogeneity to minimize spatial gradients that could influence the distribution of components within the sample. In addition, the chosen metal ion was used in a further experiment to validate the detection test's sensitivity by adding Fe3+ to a 2 ml CQDs solution at concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μM (Zhu et al., 2021a). The pH of the CQD-containing solution was maintained at ∼6.4 prior to Fe3+ detection to minimize the impact of pH fluctuations on the CQD's performance, despite such fluctuations being reported to have minimal effect (Zhao et al., 2020).

The preparation of CQDs is well documented to be significantly impacted by retention time. Under ultraviolet (UV) light with a wavelength of 365 nm, M-CQD aqueous solutions glow brightly with a yellowish-green tint. As shown in Fig. S1, as the hydrothermal reaction period increases, the produced CQD solution grows brighter under irradiation with UV, suggesting an increased concentration of CQDs. With prolonged heating at high temperatures, the colour darkens. These findings align with previous studies, suggesting that prolonged heating at high temperatures led to complete carbonization, ultimately resulting in CQD agglomeration (Ahn et al., 2019).

To catalyse the depolymerization of biopolymers into monomers and enable CQD synthesis at a lower temperature, it may be necessary to utilize an additional solvent or oxidants; in this instance, we used an organic acid. The reaction requires a significant amount of time because it requires the aromatic lignin monomers to undergo acidolysis, dehydration, and finally condensation (Papaioannou et al., 2019). Over time, carbon-carbon bonds replaced the oxygenated groups. However, even though the reaction kinetics are slower at lower temperatures (less than 180 °C), too much time spent in the reaction might also result in a reduction in the size of the CQDs, as was observed by Quaid et al. (2022). At the optimum excitation wavelength, PL spectra were obtained to display the emission photoluminescent spectra of each sample (Fig. 1 A). The samples' optimal fluorescence intensities ranged from 170.53 to 999.82, with the M-CQD12h displaying the highest fluorescence and so being chosen as the ideal hydrothermal reaction time for the subsequent experiment with acid additive addition, as described in Section 3.1.2 .

UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to detect the electronic transition in M-CQD12h, and the result is shown in Fig. 1B. Within the range of 250–550 nm, the M-CQD12h exhibited a wide absorption band peaking at 260 nm, suggesting a π-π* transition within its structure. This transition likely stems from the presence of sp2 CC bonds in the aromatic framework of CQDs (Zhao et al., 2020). The observation of a much weaker absorption peak in the vicinity of 280 nm suggested that M-CQD12h displayed an n-π* transition. This transition may be associated with the CO bonds that are present in CQDs, representing carbonyl, carboxyl, and aldehyde groups (Si et al., 2018). Another wear adsorption shoulder at 380 nm is assigned to the heteroatom-doped surface state or the energy trap caused by the n-π* transition of functional groups, representing the functionalized substituent of the acid additive (2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid) on the resultant CQDs’ surface (Zhu et al., 2021a).

We can see the obtained PL spectra for M-CQD12h in Fig. 1C. The fluorescence gradually became more intense and then attenuated when the excitation wavelength was raised from 280 to 460 nm. The optimal emission peak of fluorescence is centered at 530 nm, with excitation wavelengths of 400 nm. Furthermore, as the excitation wavelength increased, the emission wavelength of red shifted, demonstrating excitation wavelength dependency. The giant red-edge effect, a result of polar groups' solvent relaxation on the CQD graphene-oxide structure in polar solvents like water, could potentially explain this finding (Cushing et al., 2014). This phenomenon is further elaborated upon in Section 3.3 . The fluorescence performance of M-CQD12h is evaluated by measuring the quantum yield (QY) at the 400 nm excitation wavelength. The QY increased to 17.7% after a 12-h hydrothermal reaction, as shown in Fig. 1D, but further extension of the reaction to 18 and 24 h had a lesser effect. The competing reaction between hydrolysis and repolymerization may be limiting the reaction rates that may take place (Quaid et al., 2022). The QY of 1.5-h lignin-derived CQDs is low, and they emit hardly negligible fluorescence. This is likely due to the insoluble nature of lignin in water and its high chemical stability following moderate hydrothermal treatment (Zhu et al., 2021a).

Practically, all fluorescing molecules have conjugated double-bond systems, and the greater the conjugated system, the easier it is to excite the delocalized π electrons, and hence the simpler it emits photons (Williams and Bridges, 1964). Therefore, luminous compounds, such as CQDs, often include a sizable percentage of aromatic or heterocyclic rings. As a result, expanding the conjugated double-bond systems is a viable strategy for boosting CQD fluorescence. Electron-donating substituents also have a significant impact on the efficacy of fluorophores, including –NH2, –NHR, –NR2, –OH, –OR, or –CN. When a fluorophore absorbs light, it not only excites its electrons, but it also transports the n-electrons of the substituents, positioned outside the ring, to the ring. Substituents and the conjugated bond of CQD's aromatic ring share an electron cloud due to their nearly parallel electron cloud orientations, which strengthens the system of mutual conjugated double bonds between them. Substituents that donate electrons were also included, leading to a p-π electron-donating conjugation with the benzene ring, which increased the π-electron conjugation even further. Consequently, Zhao et al. (2021) functionalized the surface of the lignin-derived CQDs by introducing electron-donating substituents, thereby increasing the fluorescence emission.

Many different organisms, including plants, animals, and even bacteria, contain organic acids. These acids are typically weak and only partly dissociate; they are safe, efficient, and ecologically beneficial (Gerold et al., 2022). Zhu et al. (2021b) demonstrated that, despite their lower acidity, organic acids, particularly those with >1 heteroatom configuration, may serve as effective oxidizing agents in the production of CQDs, replacing mineral acids. In this study, 2,4-diaminobenzenesulphonic acid (C6H8N2O3S) functions as an oxidizing agent; its N and S heteroatoms are primarily responsible for its oxidizing properties. Acidolysis at high temperatures, where β-O-4 linkages cleave in the effort for lignin depolymerization, introduces negatively charged oxygen-containing moieties (e.g., C–O, CO, or O–H) to the surface of the resultant CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Aromatization of CQDs is considered to take place via the synthesis of aromatic clusters by dehydration, which readily occurs in acidic conditions, followed by aldol condensation and cycloaddition (Nallayagari et al., 2022). Simultaneously, electron-donating substituents (NH2 and SO3H), together with a homocyclic ring that serves as a conjugated double-bond system of the 2,4-diaminobenzenesulphonic acid compound, were covalently grafted onto existing hydroxyl groups on the surface of the resultant CQDs via an epoxy ring-opening reaction (Qiao et al., 2016). Fig. S2 vividly displays the green fluorescence of M-CQD when exposed to UV light (365 nm).

Increasing dopant concentration often increases the fluorescence of CQDs. This occurs because a higher acid concentration leads to more electron donor groups and conjugated double-bond systems building up on the CQDs produced, hence intensifying M-CQD fluorescence. This is because the acid dopant may contribute to electronic mobility by means of the functional groups it contains, such as hydroxyl (-OH) groups or amino (-NH2) groups, which can serve as electron donors (Azami et al., 2023). These functional groups can donate lone pairs of electrons to nearby carbon atoms within the aromatic systems of CQDs, allowing for the development of conjugated double bonds. Such carbon structure formation confers the CQDs with improved electronic characteristics, among which is fluorescence.

As can be seen in Fig. 3 A, the intensity of the M-CQD fluorescence increased from 266.79 to 999.82 as the concentration of the acid increased, which corresponds to an increase in QY from 4.63 to 17.7% (Fig. 3B). All spectra's excitation wavelengths are centered at 530 nm, despite the variations in acid content. The M-CQD0.05M fluorescence was the brightest at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm; however, between 420 and 500 nm, it gradually diminished (Fig. 3C). This decline can be attributed to the limited number of unoccupied hydroxyl groups on the M-CQD, which restricts potential reactions. Specifically, the grafting of 2,4-diaminobenzenesulphonic acid onto the CQD's surface is constrained by the availability of hydroxyl groups.

The red shift in the spectra, which corresponds to the presence of second peaks or a buffer at excitation wavelengths greater than 440 nm, was indicative of the interaction between the fluorophore's polar functional groups (-OH and –COOH) and the solvent polarity, which resulted in low-energy fluorescence emission (Zhao et al., 2021). Fig. 3D shows that raising the acid concentration from 0.05 M to 0.1 M reached a maximum for the M-CQD fluorescence intensity and QY, which then subsided at higher acid concentrations. Thus, among all the samples, the M-CQD displayed the brightest fluorescence at a concentration of 0.05 M acid, leading to its selection for further characterization.

There is considerable variation in hydrolysis rates during the hydrothermal reaction between different biopolymers. At temperatures as low as 180 °C, hemicelluloses and cellulose hydrolyze, whereas lignin depolymerizes at temperatures higher than 260 °C (Paksung et al., 2020). Nevertheless, monomers do not dissociate until temperatures approach 275–280 °C (Huang et al., 2017). Therefore, CQDs made from lignocellulosic biomass exhibit temperature-dependent characteristics. To assess M-CQD's performance, the PL of M-CQD with varying hydrothermal temperatures (110–230 °C) is measured at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The brightness of the solution under UV irradiation increases as the hydrothermal temperature rises (Fig. S3), indicating a higher concentration of CQDs. This was affirmed with the PL spectra generated, whereby the samples' optimal fluorescence intensities ranged from 223.64 to 999.82, with the M-CQD200°C displaying the highest fluorescence (Fig. 4 A Previous studies' speculation that inadequate reactions occur at low temperatures is supported by our findings (Zhu et al., 2021a). As the reaction temperature is elevated from 110 to 200 °C, the QY increases from 3.93 to 17.7%; however, additional increases in hydrothermal temperature to 230 °C have no effect on the QYs (Fig. 4D). Lignin-derived CQDs produced at 110 °C have practically no fluorescence or QY. As shown in Fig. 4B the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 core is responsible for the 200–300 nm absorption peak seen in UV–vis absorption spectra, while the n-π* transition of CO at the surface is responsible for the weak absorption shoulder at 280 nm (Zhao et al., 2020). Most notably, M-CQD hydrothermalized at 200 °C had a higher absorbance than those hydrothermalized at lower temperatures, suggesting a higher concentration of CC groups in their carbon core. There was a slight red shifting in the spectra, as per Fig. 4C.

Recent research on CQD concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity has disproved the long-held belief that increasing CQD concentration results in a corresponding increase in CQD fluorescence intensity (Y. Zhang et al., 2019). Contrary to the expectation of a linear increase, Wang et al. (2018) posit that particle-size spacing plays a pivotal role in modulating CQD fluorescence. At lower concentrations, enlarged particle spacing restricts interparticle interactions, prompting a red shift in PL and heightened intensity under intensified ultraviolet excitation. Conversely, at higher concentrations, CQD aggregation alters the intrinsic state of the particles (Ba et al., 2020). This aggregation leads to electron redistribution and distortion of oxygen-related functional groups, resulting in broader photon absorption bands and a red shift in PL emission centers. Despite significant variations in optical characteristics across CQD solutions of different concentrations, increasing CQD concentration remains an effective strategy for enhancing PL in purified CQDs.

The experiment used solutions containing 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0 mg mL−1 M-CQD (designated as M-CQD0.125–1.0 mg/ml, respectively). The hydrothermal reaction was fixed at 200 °C for 12 h using a 0.05 M acid additive concentration. Fig. 5 A displays the results of generating emission spectra at the optimum excitation wavelength for each sample. Upon further analysis, we indeed observed indications of M-CQD aggregation at higher concentrations, which contributed to the decrease in QY. Elevated concentrations increase the probability of CQDs coming into close proximity, leading to intermolecular interactions and aggregation phenomena. This aggregation had resulted in non-radiative pathways, such as energy transfer or quenching, leading to a decrease in QY.

These findings are consistent with the observations of Shao et al. (2017), as the M-CQD concentration decreased, the maximum emission wavelengths progressively red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelengths, and the fluorescence first strengthened and then diminished (Fig. 5C). There was a wide range in the fluorescence intensity from 318.52 to 999.82, with the brightest fluorescence occurring by M-CQD0.5 mg/ml at a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, hence the highest QY (Fig. 5D) As a result, we concluded that a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 was the cut-off point for M-CQD concentration in our subsequent study in Section 3.3 .

We investigated M-CQD fluorescence at several pH levels. Under UV light irradiation, M-CQD solutions glow brightest in the pH range of 6.0–8.0 but dim in response to excessively acidic or alkaline conditions, as seen visually (Fig. 6 A). This is consistent with the PL emission spectra shown in Fig. 6C, where the intensities peak in M-CQD solution with a pH range of 6.0–8.0 but vanish under severely acidic or alkaline environments, indicating that their QY values are pH-dependent (Fig. 6D). At high pH, the PL emission intensity is dampened by the accumulation of negative charge densities and the suppression of π-π* and n-π* transitions (Liu et al., 2019). The binding of protons to the emissive sites of M-CQD may be responsible for the red shift in the fluorescence spectra seen at high pH. Hydrothermal treatment aided by 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid can dope carbon materials with N and S atoms, leading to the formation of N and S-containing groups on the carbon materials' surface (such as nitrous and sulfuric anhydride, respectively) and an increase in negative charge density. This can improve the materials' protonation-deprotonation performance, particularly in response to changes in pH, and hence their photoluminescence behaviors (Liu et al., 2021). Functional groups on the surface of M-CQD (e.g., carboxyl groups, nitrous anhydride, and sulfuric anhydride) undergo protonation and deprotonation, which yields M-CQD its pH-responsive capacity (F. Yang et al., 2018).

The absorption spectra of M-CQD, modified with N and S dopants in the form of 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, exhibit a significant variance when compared to those of B-CQD (refer to Fig. S4A). The UV–vis spectra-derived band gaps, illustrated in Fig. S4B, reveal that B-CQD's bandgap energy (Eg) is 4.14 eV. On the other hand, we calculate the bandgap of M-CQD, decorated with the acid additive, to be 4.08 eV. This calculation indicates a reduction in the band gap due to the incorporation of N and S dopants, thereby enhancing M-CQD's fluorescence capability.

A reduced band gap implies that less energy is required to promote electrons to the conduction band, making M-CQD more susceptible to excitation. This suggests that the incorporation of N and S dopants created new energy states within the band structure, facilitating the absorption of lower-energy photons and subsequently emitting higher-energy photons during fluorescence. Additionally, the introduction of dopants may create defect sites in the lattice, disrupting the perfect hexagonal arrangement of carbon atoms. These defects act as energy levels in the CQD's bandgap, providing additional pathways for electron transitions. During the absorption of photons, electrons can be excited from the valence band to these defect states, and subsequently, during relaxation, they emit photons as they return to the ground state.

As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 7 A–C), the M-CQD appears zero-dimensional and discrete quasi-spherical, with a mean diameter of 5.22 ± 1.3 nm. HR-TEM imaging also reveals a structure with enhanced atom resolution comparable to that of graphite (002) crystal plane with a lattice spacing of 0.218 nm, as found in prior research on biomass-derived CQDs. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7 D) reveal characteristic stretching vibration peaks at 3000–3407, 1610, 1531, 1481, 1410, 1310, 1200, 1115, 866, 777, and 620 cm−1 for the –OH, aromatic CC, –NH (secondary amide), –CH3 (sp3 carbon), –CH2 (sp2 carbon), C–N, SO, OSO group, SO (S-phenyl group), C–C (deformation vibration), and –NH (primary amide), respectively. In particular, one can readily observe the SO of the sulphonic group, the OSO of sulphonated aromatic diamines, and the –NH vibration peak of the -C–NH–C group in the spectra of M-CQD, which originate from the sulfonic and amino groups of acid additives (Ahmad Farid et al., 2023c; Jing et al., 2019). The absence of N and S-based functional groups in the spectrum of non-doped CQD (B-CQD), as shown in Fig. S5 (D), demonstrates the successful addition of acid additive functionalization to the CQD produced.

Fig. 7 (E) depicts thermograms from TGA and DTG, which demonstrate weight loss as a function of temperature and its derivative. Evaporation of adsorbed moisture is responsible for the weight loss of M-CQD (5 wt%) up to 140 °C. Temperatures over 240 °C remove oxygen functional groups and acid additive groups from the graphene surface, leading to a subsequent 15 wt% weight loss (Belessi et al., 2019). We studied the carbon-crystalline structure of CQDs by obtaining XRD patterns and Raman spectra. In Fig. 7 (F), the (002) diffraction peak of CQDs is shown to be located at 2θ = 21.6°, which corresponds to that 0.209 nm interlayer spacing. The transition from amorphous lignin to highly crystalline CQDs causes the peak to shift and narrow. We used Raman spectroscopy to measure the degree of graphitization in CQDs (Fig. 7 G). The sp3 hybridized carbon atoms are related to the D peak at 1340 cm−1, whereas the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms are associated with the G peak at 1588 cm−1. The ID/IG ratio of the disordered D peak and ordered G peak is 0.99, indicating defect density in CQDs comparable to graphite's systematic structure. The ID/IG ratio exhibited a decrease to 0.97 relative to the non-doped CQD (B-CQD), as evidenced in Fig. S5 (E). The increased D-peak intensity in the M-CQD spectrum confirmed the reduction in aromatic carbon composition resulting from the acid additive's functionalization.

We further describe the surface functional groups of CQDs using XPS (Fig. 8 ). M-CQD's wide XPS spectra exhibit strong signals of carbon (C) at 283.6 eV and oxygen (O) at 530.6 eV, and moderate peaks of nitrogen (N) at 402.6 eV or sulphur (S) at 167.6 eV. M-CQD is representative of N- and S-co-doped lignin-derived CQDs. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra reveal the existence of sp2 carbon (C–C/CC) at 284.8 eV, sp3 carbon (C–O) at 286.3 eV, and carbonyl groups (CO) at 288.4 eV in the M-CQD (Lim Teik Zheng et al., 2021). The CO concentration, due to its electron-accepting feature, is considered the most influential component in determining the emission wavelength of CQDs (Li et al., 2021).

The high-resolution N1s spectra of M-CQD represent the amine-N and C–N groups as deconvolution peaks at 401.9 and 399.5 eV, respectively. Blue-shift PL emission behaviour has been linked to C–N in the form of pyridine-N and pyrrole-N as electron donors in the electron delocalization system (Holá et al., 2017). Deconvoluted peaks at 168.7 (2p 3/2) and 169.7 (2p 1/2) eV in the high-resolution S2p spectrum of M-CQD are typical of the acid additive's sulphonic acid (-SO3H). The quantitative examination of the XPS peaks of M-CQD reveals a composition of 45.93% carbon, 41.46% oxygen, 7.04% nitrogen, and 5.55% sulphur. This indicates that the M-CQD has a high degree of surface oxidation (O/C ratio = 0.90), which may have prompted a red shift in the PL emission wavelengths.

In order to place the results in the wider scientific literature, the selection of these specific metal ions was influenced by existing research on the selectivity of CQDs (Zhao et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021a). The selectivity of M-CQD towards various metal ions at 500 μM concentration was investigated by analyzing the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F/F0) when exposed to Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, or Fe3+. According to the data shown in Fig. 9 A, Fe3+ was found to significantly reduce the initial fluorescence intensity of CQDs by 95%, but other metal ions did not have a noticeable effect on fluorescence. This decision was in agreement with the prior findings indicating the exceptional efficacy of lignin-derived CQDs in Fe3+ detection, with a quenching efficiency exceeding 90% at 0.1 M metal ion concentration (Zhao et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021a). Moreover, previous studies supported our rationale to concentrate solely on Fe3+, as metal ions with a 1+ and 2+ charge typically have little to no effect on the quenching of PL intensity compared to their 3+ counterparts (Zhu et al., 2021a). This rapid chelation between Fe3+ ions and CQDs, compared to other metal ions, may also account for the high selectivity of CQDs for Fe3+ detection, according to prior investigation (Liu et al., 2015b). Therefore, it can be inferred that the enhanced quenching effectiveness is a result of the stronger contacts formed by the highly charged Fe3+ ions with the negatively charged or polarizable sites on the M-CQD.

Regarding the mechanism, research has shown that the quenching of CQDs by Fe³⁺ ions might exhibit variability. The postulated inner filter effect (IFE), as suggested by Yang et al. (2019), plays a crucial role. In this process, Fe³⁺ ions absorb either the excitation light before it reaches the N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (primary IFE) or the emitted light from the N, S co-doped CQDs (secondary IFE). This absorption leads to a decrease in the observed fluorescence intensity. Another commonly acknowledged process is static or dynamic quenching, where the excited states of the CQDs are deactivated non-radiatively either when Fe³⁺ ions form complexes or collide with one other, respectively (Qi et al., 2019). R. Zhang et al. (2020) presented a feasible route of complexation between sensor CQDs and Fe3+ ions by the production of ring-opened Fe3+ complexes driven by Fe3+. This complex formation was proven using Job's plot. In addition, the disparity in electronegativity between Fe³⁺ ions and N-CQDs leads to a redistribution of charges, which disrupts the electronic structure of the N-CQDs and alters their surface states. These surface states play a crucial role in the emission of photoluminescence (PL) (Ali et al., 2023). Additionally, the presence of Fe3+ may induce an oxidation-reduction interaction with the CQDs, hence enhancing the process of fluorescence quenching (Q. Zhang et al., 2020). The transfer of electrons occurs from the excited states of the CQDs to Fe³⁺ ions, resulting in the reduction of Fe³⁺ to Fe2⁺ and the formation of non-fluorescent complexes. The direct electronic interactions efficiently suppress the fluorescence of CQDs by depleting their excited states.

In our analysis, we observed a decrease in fluorescence intensity as the concentration of Fe3+ increased, indicating the selective quenching of CQDs by Fe3+, as seen in Fig. 9F. The presence of oxygen-functional groups, particularly carboxyl and hydroxyl moieties, in our synthesized CQDs facilitated the formation of non-fluorescent complexes with Fe3+ ions at the edges of CQDs. The observed spatial quenching pattern of CQDs in the presence of Fe3+, seemingly quenching from the bottom upward, may have resulted from the positioning of the UV light source at the top of the vial during image capture. However, as stated in Section 2.6 , we took adequate precautionary measures during PL measurements to minimize these artifacts, thereby ensuring the accuracy of our measurements and the reliability of data interpretation.

We assessed the sensitivity of CQDs to Fe3+ detection by adding Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μM to an aqueous CQD solution. Fig. 9C shows that the PL intensities of the resulting mixtures decreased as the concentration of Fe3+ increased, indicating the extreme sensitivity of the concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching, thereby reducing the QYs (see Fig. 9D). At a dosage of 500 μM, Fe3+ effectively suppressed 95% of the quantum yield of the M-CQD. However, its efficacy showed a two-fold increase compared to undoped CQD (B-CQD), with the maximum QY measured at a modest 8.65, as shown in Fig. S5B. Additionally, the attenuation induced by Fe3+ reached 95% at a minimal dosage of 400 μM. The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9803, indicating a strong relationship between the 1-(F0/F) value (F0 and F indicate the PL intensities with and without metal ions, respectively) and Fe3+ concentrations below 300 μM (refer to Fig. 9E). Assuming a linear correlation between the 1-(F0/F) value and Fe3+ concentrations, the calculated limit of detection (LOD) is remarkably low at 0.15 μM (3.3 x δ/m), where δ represents the standard deviation of the intercept and m signifies the slope of the linear fitting line.

Based on the observed quenching pattern, our scenario is consistent with Deng et al. (2018) and Liu et al. (2015a), suggesting that the quenching effect remains constant due to the formation of coordination complexes between the oxygen-containing functional groups of CQDs and Fe3+ ions, and this strong selectivity observed may be due to the quicker chelation of Fe3+ ions with CQDs compared to other ions. The oxygen functional groups, particularly hydroxyl, present in our synthesized M-CQD act as electron donors and form non-fluorescent complexes at the edges of the CQDs. The proposed model of static quenching, as described by Ru et al. (2019), is illustrated in Fig. 10 . This suggests that the fluorescence quenching may result from an electronic transition from the excited state of the CQDs to the d-orbital of Fe3+ ions (Sahoo et al., 2020). Fe3+ ions have an electron deficiency in their outermost d-orbital, which gives them a strong oxidizing state and a greater ability to accept electrons, leading to a significant decrease in PL intensity. Fe3+ ions may reduce to Fe2+ in an aqueous solution in the presence of CQDs, leading to fluorescence quenching (Zhu et al., 2021a).

The M-CQD nanoprobe demonstrates a remarkable limit of detection (LOD) for Fe³⁺, which is consistent with the values reported in scientific literature and is lower than the Fe³⁺ concentration threshold suggested by the World Health Organization for drinking water (5.36 μM) (Jayaweera et al., 2019). Table 1 shows that the M-CQD performs similarly to other nanoprobes produced from biomass over the whole detection range. Nevertheless, it is important to acknowledge that the limit of detection (LOD) of the M-CQD exhibits lower sensitivity compared to other well-established Fe³⁺ detection probes. The LODs (limit of detection) for the chemical tests and instrumental methodologies mentioned in lines 710–720 are as follows: 0.01 μM using the phenanthroline method (Kawakubo et al., 2004), 0.07 μM via HPLC (Ichinoki et al., 2009), 0.1 μM through AAS (Moldovan and Neagu, 2002), 0.1 μM by fluorometric measurement (Teixeira et al., 2002), and as low as 0.001 μM with flow injection analysis (Yaqoob et al., 2006).

This constraint affects the findings of our study by emphasizing that while the M-CQD nanoprobe is efficient for environmental and practical uses, it may not be the optimal option for detecting ultra-low levels of Fe³⁺ where greater sensitivity is necessary. Established techniques have a greater sensitivity, allowing them to detect even lower amounts of Fe³⁺. This is particularly important in situations when very low detection limits are required. However, the practical importance of the M-CQD nanoprobe remains substantial. The sensitivity and selectivity of the detection method are enough for identifying Fe³⁺ in a range of applications, such as environmental monitoring, industrial control, and water quality evaluation. These applications do not always need the very low detection limits offered by more sensitive technologies. An application of the M-CQD nanoprobe is the efficient monitoring of Fe³⁺ in effluent to provide quality control and implement safety measures, within its specified sensitivity range. This feature guarantees that the technique remains very beneficial for timely interventions to uphold water quality and assure safety in industrial operations and medical diagnostics.

CQDs hold great promise for various applications, including sensor development, due to their straightforward synthesis, low toxicity, and high optical quality. While previous studies primarily utilized readily available lignin, we extended this investigation to explore the effectiveness of biomass-sourced lignin instead. By doing so, we aim to provide a nuanced analysis of the potential of biomass-derived CQDs as nanoprobe materials, laying the groundwork for future research on optimizing biomass-derived CQD-based sensing platforms for diverse environmental and industrial applications.

In our study, we utilized a mild organic acid additive, 2,4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, to introduce electron-donating substituents (N and S dopants) to the surface of bamboo lignin-derived carbon dots. Physicochemical characterization revealed that the resulting M-CQD exhibited a lamellar quasi-spherical structure with a mean diameter of 5.22 ± 1.3 nm and an interlayer spacing of 0.209 nm. The results confirmed the incorporation of sulfonic and amino groups onto the carbon dots, along with oxygen-containing functional groups. Under UV illumination, the M-CQD emitted bright green light, with the optimal emission peak centered at 530 nm. The introduction of N and S dopants resulted in enhanced fluorescence, as evidenced by the reduced bandgap energy (Eg). The fluorescence intensity reached its peak at a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, with a measured QY of 17.7%, and remained stable between pH 6.0 and 8.0. Although it lacks sensitivity compared to other established Fe probes, M-CQD exhibits comparable performance to other biomass-derived nanoprobes, achieving a LOD as low as 0.15 μM, surpassing the WHO's detection limit for Fe detection in drinking water.

Our study demonstrates the dual-purpose use of converting biomass waste into valuable carbon fluorophores and utilizing them as biosensors for easy Fe3+ detection in water. This innovative approach highlights the potential for waste utilization and environmental monitoring to occur simultaneously. However, the M-CQD's sensitivity falls short compared to established Fe probes, hindering its ability to detect and quantify low concentrations accurately. Further research is crucial to address analytical challenges across different sample matrices. Additionally, optimizing or enhancing M-CQD is essential to achieve sensitivity levels similar to conventional Fe probes.

Mohammed Abdillah Ahmad Farid: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jacqueline Lease: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Andou Yoshito: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
